title: Quantum Global Variational Learning for Quantum Error Correction
abstract: Efficient quantum error correction is essential for the advancement of quantum computing. We propose a quantum neural network with a global structure that reduces the number of unitary matrices required in quantum circuits. This approach resulted in a 97\% reduction in training time and up to a 25\% improvement in the training completion rate, ultimately achieving a 100\% success rate in training while surpassing the error correction performance reported in previous studies. In addition, we demonstrated the enhanced robustness of quantum error correction against internal network noise. Moreover, the fidelity of quantum error correction under internal network noise increased by up to 15\% due to the reduced computational load.
summary: This paper introduces Quantum Global Variational Learning (QGVL) as an efficient alternative to standard QAE methods for Quantum Error Correction. By consolidating local unitaries into a global structure, QGVL drastically reduces training complexity and time while maintaining high fidelity in correcting errors across various qubit codes. It demonstrates that this ML approach can achieve performance comparable to established theoretical stabilizer codes.
main_problem: Developing efficient Quantum Error Correction (QEC) methods that avoid classical measurements and overcome training convergence issues inherent in large quantum neural networks.
main_result: The proposed Quantum Global Variational Learning (QGVL) approach significantly reduces the number of required unitary parameters compared to standard QAE, leading to substantial improvements in training time and robustness against internal network noise while matching established stabilizer code performance.
method: The authors propose using a global structure within a quantum neural network framework for error correction. Training involves minimizing infidelity by optimizing trainable unitary matrices via gradient descent techniques adapted for quantum circuits.
model_or_system: The system models qubits susceptible to various noise channels, including Pauli X errors and general depolarizing noise. QEC is tested using established codes like the 3-qubit repetition code, 5-qubit, Steane (7-qubit), and Shor (9-qubit) stabilizer codes.
key_observables: Mean fidelity of error correction output vs. input error rate ($p$), training time reduction (e.g., 80% for 3-qubit), and success rate in achieving convergence.
important_parameters: Error rates ($p$, $p_n$), number of qubits ($n=3, 5, 7, 9$), and network structure parameters ($m, n$).
main_assumptions: The analysis often assumes that noise affecting different quantum states is equally probable when modeling internal network noise. The comparison relies on comparing QGVL performance against established algorithmic stabilizer codes.
figures_summary: Figures illustrate fidelity dependence on error rate for various qubit counts (3, 5, 7, 9) comparing QGVL to stabilizer codes; diagrams show the layered QNN architecture and the global unitary mapping structure.
paper_structure: The paper progresses from establishing the need for efficient QEC over measurement-based methods. It contrasts Quantum Autoencoders (QAE) with the proposed Global Variational Learning (QGVL), demonstrating QGVL's parameter efficiency, and finally validates its performance across increasing qubit counts and complex noise models.
why_interesting: For AMO physicists studying open quantum systems, this work provides a computational framework to model how machine learning can be used to design or optimize error mitigation protocols for physical hardware limited by decoherence.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: Figure 1: Mapping from DQNN layer k −1 to layer k. The interlayer mapping in the Quantum Autoencoder (QAE) is illustrated. First, the tensor product of the density operator ρk−1 and nk ancillary states |0⟩is con- structed, representing the expansion of the quantum in- formation from layer k−1 together with the initial states |0⟩assigned to each node in layer k. Subsequently, nk unitary operations U j k are applied sequentially to ρk−1 and the ancillary states, where each Uj k corresponds to an edge connecting layer k −1 to the j-th node in layer k. After all unitary operations are applied, the combined system of nk−1 + nk qubits is reduced to nk qubits by performing a partial trace over the...

Figure 4: Quantum Autoencoder (QAE) network archi- tecture consisting of 3-1-3 qubits. The network outputs noise-free quantum information ρout from a 3-qubit in- put quantum state. The encoder and decoder corre- spond to the first and second interlayer mappings, re- spectively, each acting on four qubits. The trainable parameters of the network are the unitary matrices U1 and U2 associated with these interlayer mappings.

Figure 5: Fidelity between target quantum states and error-corrected quantum states obtained using the 3- qubit QGVL (solid line), compared with the fidelity in the case of a single-qubit error among three qubits (dot- ted line), and the fidelity achieved by algorithmic correc- tion using the stabiliser code (black dots). The target quantum states were generated from 104 randomly pre- pared quantum states encoded with the repetition code. The input quantum states were produced by introducing bit-flip noise in accordance with the error rates indicated on the x-axis.

Figure 6: Fidelity obtained by the 3-qubit QGVL from 102 test data with a bit-flip error rate of p = 0.1. Results for physical qubits and stabiliser codes are also shown for comparison. The fidelity of noiseless or fully corrected quantum target states is plotted at 1. In contrast, the fidelity of noisy physical qubits is 0, whereas the fideli- ties of logical qubits after error correction operations are distributed within the range (0.0, 1.0).

Figure 7: Quantum Process Tomography (QPT) results without any operation (a), using QGVL (b), and employing the stabiliser code (c). The input basis comprised the set {|0⟩, |1⟩}⊗3, and the measurement operators were drawn from {I, X, Z, Y }⊗3. Observables correspond to Pauli operators including: III, IIZ, IZI, IZZ, ZII, ZIZ, ZZI, and ZZZ.
